Category: Hospitality & Tourism: Hotels, Tourism, Travel Agencies, Food & Beverage
Description: Hidden risks in booking engines, POS, and CRM tools.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with common hospitality software such as booking engines, point-of-sale systems, or CRM tools is helpful, though no technical or AI background is required to follow this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the hidden risks embedded within AI-powered booking engines, point-of-sale systems, and CRM tools commonly used across the hospitality industry. You will also be able to explain how vendor AI operates within these platforms and apply practical strategies to assess and manage the associated risks within your organisation.

lessons.Description: Many hospitality businesses rely on booking engines, point-of-sale systems, and CRM tools that now include built-in AI features, often without staff realising it. These vendor-supplied AI tools can make automatic decisions about pricing, customer communications, and data handling, which can introduce risks that are easy to overlook. This lesson explores how AI is quietly embedded within the systems your team already uses every day, and what that means for your business. You will learn to identify where these hidden AI features sit, understand the potential risks they carry, and feel more confident asking the right questions of your technology suppliers. No prior knowledge of AI is required, just a willingness to look more closely at the tools your business depends on.
lessons.content: Hidden risks in booking engines, POS, and CRM tools.

slides.slidetitle: AI Already Lives in Your Systems
slides.subtitle: Recognising the hidden technology in everyday tools
slides.slidecontent:
• Booking engines, POS, and CRM tools contain built-in AI
• Most staff don't realise these features exist
• Vendors add AI as standard without fanfare
• Daily operations continue while AI works behind the scenes
• Understanding starts with awareness

slides.subtitle: How booking engines make real-time decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• Algorithms adjust rates and availability automatically
• Different guests see different prices
• Pricing factors: location, device, browsing history
• No staff notifications or decision logs
• Configuration errors affect multiple bookings

slides.subtitle: Where guest information goes and consent gets buried
slides.slidecontent:
• Guest data leaves your property management system
• Consent settings hidden in configuration menus
• Algorithmic bias affects pricing fairness
• Compliance audits rarely examine AI behaviour
• Data processing often lacks transparency

slides.subtitle: Common operational problems with AI booking systems
slides.slidecontent:
• Silent price updates without alerts
• Incorrect room assignments confirmed automatically
• Overbooking through poor channel synchronisation
• Misfired discount rules applying wrong rates
• Guest profiles mixed up with wrong personal data

slides.subtitle: Identifying where algorithms are already working
slides.slidecontent:
• Automated rate adjustments based on demand
• Chatbots handling guest inquiries
• CRM systems predicting customer behaviour
• POS fraud detection and unusual transaction alerts
• Personalised loyalty programme rewards

slides.subtitle: Warning signs before signing contracts
slides.slidecontent:
• Vague 'AI-powered' claims without clear explanations
• No transparency about data usage or storage
• Pricing changes based on uncontrolled model updates
• No staff override options for AI decisions
• Case studies showing only best-case scenarios

slides.subtitle: Actions to take control of vendor AI exposure
slides.slidecontent:
• Question vendors directly about AI features and data access
• Review contracts for third-party data sharing clauses
• Conduct regular guest data audits
• Train staff to question AI suggestions
• Maintain logs of AI-enabled systems and review dates

slides.subtitle: Essential principles for ongoing AI management
slides.slidecontent:
• AI exists in your current systems, not future ones
• Regular vendor questioning builds transparency
• Each platform type carries distinct risks
• Staff awareness provides practical defence
• Start by reviewing one contract for AI clauses this week

scripts.script: Let's start with a fundamental reality: AI isn't coming to hospitality systems, it's already here. Your booking engine, point-of-sale system, and CRM tools likely contain AI features that work quietly in the background. Most hospitality staff don't realise this because vendors typically add these capabilities as standard features without making a big announcement about it. Your team continues their daily work while algorithms make automated decisions about pricing, customer communications, and data handling. This isn't necessarily problematic, but it does mean the first step in managing AI risk is simply recognising that these technologies are already part of your operational environment.

Booking engines represent one of the most common places where hospitality businesses encounter vendor AI, particularly through dynamic pricing algorithms. When guests search for rooms on your website, the system often adjusts rates and availability in real time based on factors like their geographic location, the type of device they're using, or their browsing history. This means different guests can see completely different prices for the same room. The challenge here is transparency: these pricing decisions happen automatically without alerting your staff, and there's typically no log you can check to understand why the system offered a particular rate or declined a booking. When configuration errors occur, they can trigger pricing or availability mistakes that affect numerous guests before anyone notices the problem.

Beyond pricing decisions, booking engine AI creates data flow concerns that many properties don't fully understand. When these systems process guest information, that data often leaves your property management system and gets shared with the vendor's servers for analysis. The consent mechanisms for this data sharing are frequently buried in configuration settings that staff rarely examine. Additionally, the algorithms making these decisions can incorporate biases that result in certain guests being shown different options without anyone realising it's happening. Formal audits of how these AI systems actually behave are uncommon in our industry, which means compliance gaps can persist unnoticed for extended periods.

The automated nature of booking engine AI, while often helpful, creates specific operational risks that tend to surface at inconvenient moments. Price updates can happen in the background without any staff notification, sometimes at completely inappropriate times. Room assignments get confirmed incorrectly, leading to guest arrival problems. Poor synchronisation between channels can result in overbooking situations that develop silently. Discount rules can misfire and apply incorrect rates to bookings without human review. Perhaps most concerning, guest data can become mismatched, with personal details or preferences incorrectly attached to different customer profiles. These aren't theoretical concerns - they occur in real properties and typically become apparent when guests are standing at your front desk expecting something different.

To effectively manage AI risks, you need to recognise where these technologies are already operating in your daily systems. Dynamic pricing in booking engines uses demand-based algorithms to adjust room rates automatically. Customer service chatbots on your website respond to guest inquiries using natural language processing. CRM systems employ predictive analytics to identify which guests are likely to return or need attention. Point-of-sale systems use pattern recognition to flag unusual transactions that might indicate fraud or errors. Loyalty programmes utilise behavioural analysis to personalise reward offers for individual members. Understanding that these AI features are embedded in tools you're already using helps you ask better questions about their operation and potential risks.

Before signing contracts with new vendors, certain warning signs should prompt deeper investigation. Be cautious of suppliers who describe their systems as 'AI-powered' without being able to explain specifically what that means in practice or how it affects your operations. Vendors should be able to clearly describe how guest data is processed, where it's stored, and who has access to it. Watch for pricing structures that can change whenever the vendor updates their underlying models, as this makes budgeting unpredictable. Ensure your staff will have override capabilities when the AI makes decisions they need to question. Finally, be wary of case studies and references that only showcase positive outcomes without acknowledging any limitations or challenges - every technology has constraints, and vendors who won't discuss theirs may not be transparent about other important factors.

Taking control of vendor AI risks requires practical, ongoing actions rather than complex technical solutions. Start by directly asking your current vendors which features use AI and what guest data those features access - many hospitality businesses don't realise this is a reasonable question to ask. Review your existing contracts to identify clauses about data sharing with third parties or automated processing. Establish regular auditing practices for guest data to understand exactly what information you're holding and how it's being used. Train your staff to recognise that AI-generated suggestions aren't infallible and encourage them to question recommendations that seem inappropriate. Where possible, limit the amount of personal data that gets connected to AI-powered features. Maintain a simple internal log documenting which systems use AI and when you last reviewed their configurations and performance.

The key insight to carry forward is that managing vendor AI isn't about preparing for future technology - it's about taking control of systems that are already part of your operations. Develop a habit of asking direct questions about AI functionality and data usage whenever you interact with technology suppliers. Remember that each type of system - booking engines, point-of-sale, CRM tools - carries distinct risks that require specific attention. Your staff represent your most practical line of defence because they interact with these systems daily and can spot problems early. If you're unsure where to begin, pick one vendor contract this week and look for clauses mentioning AI, automated processing, or machine learning. This simple first step will help you understand what you're currently committed to and identify areas where you need more information or better protection.

Who is this for: Hotel managers, restaurant managers, front desk staff, reservation agents, operations managers, guest services coordinators, property managers, revenue managers, food and beverage managers
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Guest Services, Revenue Management, Food & Beverage, Front Office, IT, Management
